titleEn: AI’s role in agrifood biotech comes into sharper focus
titleZh: AI在农业食品生物技术中的角色日益清晰
summaryZh: 本文报道了阿姆斯特丹Hello Tomorrow峰会上专家对AI在农业食品生物技术中应用的讨论。AI在加速生物发现、降低成本和缩短周期方面展现出巨大潜力，尤其在解析复杂生物系统方面。但专家也指出，AI在个性化食品健康解决方案上可能并不适用，因为规模化生产更应关注共性而非个性。文章还提到AI工具在发现新成分（如蚕豆中的生物活性肽）方面的成功案例，并提醒创业者在追求AI驱动设计时需平衡成本与规模化。
translationZh:
上周在阿姆斯特丹举行的Hello Tomorrow峰会上，专家表示，人工智能已经在改变农业食品领域的生物技术创新，但它并非在所有地方都有用。

全球风险投资公司SOSV的普通合伙人Pae Wu在为期两天的会议的一个小组讨论中表示，近年来的事实证明，AI系统“有能力开始真正揭示生物学中的因果机制，这是我们以前无法做到的”。但尽管她和其他发言者强调了AI在生物技术发现中日益增长的重要性，他们也同样迅速地指出了该技术在该领域可能不太适用的地方。

**对个性化的怀疑**

其中一个领域是个性化生物技术食品和健康解决方案。对于硅谷的许多公司来说，代理式AI是现在和未来；让一个AI聊天机器人自由地穿梭在你的应用程序和数字活动中，它就可以开始执行针对你需求的复杂任务。Hello Tomorrow的专家表示，这种方法可能不太适用于农业食品领域。

“我不认为所有事情都应该在个人层面上进行，”比利时投资集团Biotope Ventures的负责人Annick Verween说。“对我来说健康的东西，可能对你也是健康的，”她说，即使“有些事情不会如此”，但在规模化生产时，通常更合理的做法是针对共性进行优化。“我不认为AI是我们这里必需的工具。”

SOSV的Wu表示，在某些情况下，AI“可能有助于促进规模化”，而在其他情况下，它可能会起到相反的作用。风险投资支持的创始人“必须真正考虑当你达到规模化时会发生什么”，以及实现这一目标需要什么，Wu说。这通常需要大量的营销支出。（“上那些播客可不是免费的，”她补充道。）这可能导致艰难的决定，比如在产品的AI驱动设计程度与将其推向市场的成本之间取得平衡。“从运营角度看，这些成本是否与我们（投资者）对这些解决方案所要求的个性化程度相匹配？”她说。

**加速发现引擎**

在过去几年中，AI工具一直在降低生物技术发现的成本和时间，该领域的领导者表示，这一趋势才刚刚开始。总部位于都柏林的肽类开发商Nuritas的首席执行官Nora Khaldi表示，人工智能正在帮助改变科学家处理复杂生物系统的方式。“我们最多能思考三到四个维度。生物学不是这样思考的，”她说。“生物学中有更多更多的维度，AI将帮助整合所有这些维度——并将其视为一个系统，而不是一个单一目标。”

Khaldi将她的公司的AI平台归功于识别出蚕豆中的一种生物活性成分，她称该成分在肌肉调节方面表现出比传统牛奶蛋白更好的性能。人类研究人员需要数百万年才能精确找到这种成分。
featuredReason: 本文深入探讨了AI在农业生物技术中的机遇与边界，尤其对规模化生产与个性化之间的权衡分析，对智慧畜牧行业从业者在技术选型和投资决策上具有重要参考价值。
contentFull: Artificial intelligence is already transforming biotechnology innovation in agrifood, but it isn’t useful everywhere, experts at the Hello Tomorrow Summit in Amsterdam said last week. Recent years have proved AI systems’ “capacity to start really unraveling mechanisms in causality in biology in a way that we just haven’t been able to do before,” Pae Wu, general partner at the global venture firm SOSV, said during a panel at the two-day gathering. But while she and other speakers underscored AI’s growing importance in biotech discovery, they were just as quick to point out where the technology might not work so well in the sector. Skepticism around personalization One of those areas is in personalizing biotech food and health solutions. For much of Silicon Valley, agentic AI is the present and future; let an AI chatbot wander freely across your apps and digital activities, and it can start performing complex tasks tailored to your needs. This type of approach might not map so well onto agrifood, the experts at Hello Tomorrow said. “I don’t think everything should happen on a personal level,” said Annick Verween, head of the Belgian investment group Biotope Ventures. “What is healthy for me probably is also healthy for you,” she said, and even though “some things will not be,” it usually makes more sense to optimize for commonalities when scaling up for mass production. “I do not think that AI necessarily is a tool that we need here.” SOSV’s Wu said there are situations where AI “may actually help facilitate scale” and others where it can do the reverse. Venture-backed founders “have to really think about what’s going to happen when you’re at scale” as well as what it takes to get there, Wu said. Frequently that’s substantial marketing spend. (“Getting on those podcasts is not free,” she added.) This can lead to tough decisions, like balancing the extent of a product’s AI-powered design with the expense of launching it on the market. “Operationally, do those costs line up with the level of personalization that we [investors] do require for these solutions?” she said. Revving the discovery engine AI tools have been slashing the costs and timelines of biotech discoveries for the last several years, and leaders in the space said this trend is just getting started. Nora Khaldi, CEO of the Dublin-based peptide developer Nuritas, said artificial intelligence is helping shift the way scientists tackle complex biological systems. “We think, at max, in maybe three, four dimensions. Biology doesn’t think that way,” she said. “There are many, many more dimensions within it, and AI is going to help actually integrate all that—and see it as a system rather than as one target.” Khaldi credited her company’s AI platform with identifying a bioactive ingredient in fava beans that she said showed better performance in muscle conditioning than conventional milk-derived proteins. It would have taken human researchers millions of years to pinpoint that ingredient with traditional scientific methods, she said. Other biotech entrepreneurs at the summit showed off AI promising to speed up biotech innovation. Aquit is using the technology to develop alternatives to animal antibiotics, hoping to address growing drug-resistance problems. CEO Daniela Allerbon said the Chilean startup developed an immunostimulant to provide salmon as a feed additive that protects them against a deadly bacterial infection. Germany-based ScreenSys, which competed with Aquit and five other startups in a pitch session at the event, promoted an AI-guided platform to accelerate crop breeding. But there were signs that the AI frenzy of recent years may be moderating. Several biotech finalists in the pitch round promoted ways of addressing food system challenges at scale that conspicuously didn’t leverage AI—which for a while has often seemed synonymous with scale. And each of the biotech panelists emphasized that human judgment and process (both regulatory and scientific) would continue to set necessary boundaries on innovation. “We’re not there yet where AI comes up with something and we immediately just take it to market,” Khaldi said, adding that biotechs can’t deploy scientists merely for quality assurance. When you’re creating a molecule, efficacy is just one piece of the puzzle, she said: “Is it easily formulated? Can I put it into a pasta, for example, and boil the pasta for 12 minutes?” AI can help account for these application-level criteria early on, Khaldi said, but it can’t rewire the development process altogether. “There are still a lot of proof points that you have to go through,” she said.
